derivation: Substitute m = log n: sqrt(m) is NOT O(log m), so sqrt(log n) = O(log log n) is the false claim. A is true (constants vanish: O(n log n /100) = O(n log n)); C is true (n^x = O(n^y) for x<y); D is true (2^n is not polynomially bounded, though 'nk' is a lost superscript for n^k).
Height of a TREE = number of edges on the longest root-to-deepest-leaf path = C. A is level, B is height of an arbitrary node, D is depth of a node.
OSPF is a link-state protocol and runs Dijkstra SPF; RIP/IGRP/BGP are distance-vector / path-vector (Bellman-Ford family).
Graphs may contain cycles/loops, so traversal needs a visited set; trees are acyclic. A and C are false/irrelevant; D is wrong because the traversal difference is real.
BFS uses a FIFO queue (DFS uses a stack).

Root at height 0 means k+1 levels; max nodes = sum_{i=0..k} 2^i = 2^(k+1) - 1 = B. NOTE: options A and D are character-identical ('2^k - 1'), a transcription defect; both are wrong so the key B stays unique and answerable, but option A should be repaired (original was almost certainly '2^k').
Simulated bubble sort on {2,5,1,3,4}: 4 swaps (matches the inversion count, also computed = 4). Pass1: (5,1),(5,3),(5,4) = 3 swaps; Pass2: (2,1) = 1 swap.
A DLL node holds two separate pointers (prev and next); one pointer cannot hold two addresses.
A spanning tree keeps n-1 edges, so the max removable = e - (n-1) = e - n + 1.
Insertion sort is O(n) on an already-sorted array (adaptive best case); merge/heap stay O(n log n) and naive quicksort degrades to O(n^2).
Quick sort's partition swaps non-adjacent elements and is not stable; bubble, merge and insertion sort are stable.
Hash table gives O(1) average-case lookup; tree O(log n), heap O(n), linked list O(n).
Queue, stack and array are all linear data structures, so 'All of the above'.

DFS on a graph visits every vertex and traverses every edge (adjacency-list representation): Theta(|V| + |E|). Stem is mildly garbled ('Depth First Traversal of is', missing 'a graph') but unambiguous.
